title: Three governing truths
claim_or_observation: The poem announces failed government, praise of the renowned chaste wife, and ripening past action as governing truths
notes: Verify final screenplay wording against Tamil

title: Epic belongs to three crowned kings
claim_or_observation: The work is explicitly said to belong equally to the three crowned kings
notes: Binding support for retaining the Vanji movement

title: Anklet names the epic
claim_or_observation: The fate-entwined anklet is named as the cause and gives the work its title
notes: Treat as narrative object and evidentiary system

title: Pukar merchant households
claim_or_observation: Kannaki and Kovalan are introduced through wealthy merchant lineages in celebrated Pukar
notes: Household economy requires further canto extraction

title: Literary ages stated
claim_or_observation: Kannaki is described as twelve and Kovalan as sixteen at introduction
notes: Literary age statements do not determine performer age

title: Kovalan loses wealth
claim_or_observation: Kovalan loses rare wealth through his life with a woman trained for the theatre
notes: Later cantos must supply relational and artistic complexity

title: Kannaki's anklet becomes survival property
claim_or_observation: Kovalan enters Madurai intending to sell a finely wrought anklet belonging to Kannaki
notes: Track custody and consent through full text

title: Goldsmith's prior theft
claim_or_observation: The goldsmith recognises the anklet in relation to the queen's anklet that he had stolen
notes: Do not simplify the case to innocent visual confusion

title: Execution without investigation
claim_or_observation: The Pandya does not investigate and orders the suspected thief killed and the anklet brought
notes: Central institutional-failure evidence

title: Kovalan is executed
claim_or_observation: Kovalan is slain at the execution ground before the truth is tested
notes: Sequence requires procedural mapping

title: Kannaki burns Madurai
claim_or_observation: Kannaki tears off a breast and feeds Madurai to flame after Kovalan's killing
notes: Must be staged with inhabited civic consequence

title: Government failure has moral consequence
claim_or_observation: Righteous law becomes death to rulers who fail in government
notes: Do not reduce to private vengeance

title: Past action and former-birth curse
claim_or_observation: The Madurai deity explains that an ancient deed and curse have ripened for the couple
notes: Karmic explanation must not erase present responsibility

title: Continuous prose-song architecture
claim_or_observation: The prologue inventories the cantos and describes a continuous poem interweaving prose and song
notes: Performance forms must retain narrative function

title: Madurai institutional sequence
claim_or_observation: The book proceeds through city, refuge, execution ground, suffering, city-circling, lawsuit, vow, burning and divine explanation
notes: Supports an institution-to-catastrophe dramatic spine

title: Landscapes change under severe season
claim_or_observation: Mullai and kurinji lose their ordinary nature and assume the form called palai
notes: Avoid static textbook landscape imagery

title: Road contains agricultural abundance
claim_or_observation: The route description names rice, sugarcane, millet, varaku, garlic, turmeric, plantain, areca, coconut, mango and jackfruit
notes: Food and crop identification needs Tamil and botanical review

title: Plural sacred geography
claim_or_observation: The road narrative includes multiple sacred places, deities, ascetics and traditions
notes: Do not homogenise religious worlds

title: Hill people question Kannaki
claim_or_observation: Hill people encounter the one-breasted Kannaki beneath a flowering vengai and ask who she is
notes: Their witnessing begins the social transformation

title: Kannaki names her catastrophe
claim_or_observation: Kannaki states that Madurai and its king were destroyed when past action took form and that she lost her husband
notes: Preserve her own account rather than replacing it with narration

title: Hill community adopts Kannaki as deity
claim_or_observation: The hill people call their settlement to take Kannaki as deity and establish worship through music, flowers, smoke and protective boundary
notes: Community action must not be picturesque background

title: Celestial reunion
claim_or_observation: Celestials rain flowers and take Kannaki away together with Kovalan
notes: Supernatural treatment requires theological and cinematic review

title: No exact production date
claim_or_observation: The literary and historical horizons must not be collapsed into one advertised exact year without specialist support
notes: Binding publicity and design caution

title: Anklet as audiovisual throughline
claim_or_observation: The paired anklets can organise sound, custody, memory, false identification and proof across the film
notes: Must remain subordinate to textual function

title: No minor performers in mature material
claim_or_observation: Literary age statements must not place minor performers in mature relationship, coercion or violent material
notes: Binding safeguarding rule

title: No automatic medieval visual style
claim_or_observation: Later imperial Chola and medieval temple styles must not be used as default evidence for the epic's world
notes: Requires archaeology and art-history review

title: Make justice procedure visible
claim_or_observation: The film should show how accusation moves through authority into execution so institutional failure remains legible
notes: Supported by EV-0008 through EV-0012

title: Karma is not a legal verdict
claim_or_observation: Past-action explanations must not excuse false testimony, failed investigation or summary killing
notes: Binding dialogue and publicity rule
